design_philosophy.core_loop: Pump enemies to burst them or create fault lines in terrain to collapse land masses and eliminate enemies; balance between offensive and environmental manipulation strategies
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing dual weapons (air pump vs jackhammer) while avoiding enemies and self-inflicted hazards; risk-reward of terrain destruction that can kill the player
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Strategic island demolition through environmental manipulation; gradual reduction of playable space forcing encounters with enemies
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical departure from original's free-movement maze-chase gameplay; enforces overhead grid-based island traversal with permanent, irreversible terrain modification as primary mechanic. Removes player agency in movement compared to predecessor, replaced by puzzle-like environmental destruction strategy

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with loss on enemy contact or self-destruction
difficulty.checkpointing: Round-based progression; completing round objective (three land pieces cut off or enemies self-eliminate) advances to next round
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal; difficulty escalates through round progression with increased enemy count and reduced spatial options. Limited environmental randomization. Player skill improves through pattern memorization of enemy behavior and optimal fault-line placement

progression.unlock_mechanism: Round completion triggers next island configuration with progressively shrinking playable terrain and more enemies
replayability_hooks: Bonus vegetable collection after three land pieces severed
High difficulty encourages practice to internalize enemy patterns

world_building_techniques: Island setting with water perimeter as permanent hazard
Named player character Taizo Hori carries implicit narrative thread from original

danger_signals: Fygars (dragons) kill on contact or via fire breath attack
Water perimeter is lethal environmental hazard
Terrain collapse triggered by player kills player if caught in affected zone
environmental_storytelling: Island gradually consumed by ocean as terrain is destroyed
Fault lines created by jackhammer act as visual markers of player intervention
Enemy self-elimination at round end (jumping into ocean) suggests desperation or programming limits

sources_used: wikipedia - Dig Dug II gameplay section
